title: Swarm prevention through hive management
subtitle: How to reduce swarm pressure with space, frame rotation, and timely splits.
excerpt: Swarming is the natural way a colony reproduces, but it means losing half your foraging force at the height of the season. These three proactive steps, adding space, checking for queen cells, and making a split, give the colony somewhere to put its energy instead of leaving.
sourceNotes: National Bee Unit, Managing Swarming (2024). BBKA Good Practice Guide, swarm control section. Ted Hooper, Guide to Bees and Honey (1976), chapter on swarm control. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive, swarming chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: Short orientation flights made by young bees in front of the hive, usually in the afternoon. They face the hive entrance and fly in widening arcs to memorise the hive position. Often mistaken for a swarm by new beekeepers, but the bees return rather than leaving.

A large wax cell, shaped like a peanut shell, in which the colony rears a new queen. Swarm cells are typically found along the bottom edge of the brood frame. Supersedure cells are usually built on the face of the comb.

A beekeeper-made split that mimics what happens when a natural swarm leaves. The old queen is moved with flying bees to a new box on the original stand. The original box is moved aside and a new box placed on the stand. Flying bees return to the original stand, splitting the colony without losing bees.

A queen cell built by the colony in preparation for swarming. Usually found as a batch of several cells on the lower edges and corners of brood frames. Their presence means swarm impulse is already underway.

content.text: A colony that wants to swarm gives you clear signals before it leaves. The bees fill every frame with brood and stores, leaving no space for the queen to lay. Workers start building

along the bottom bars of the brood frames. If you check weekly from mid-April, you can act before any of these cells are sealed. Once a cell is sealed, you are already in swarm-management mode rather than swarm-prevention mode.
Step 1: Add space before the colony needs it

The most reliable preventive measure is adding a super before the brood box is crowded. Check in early April. If seven or more frames are covered with bees and the colony is drawing comb to the edges of the outer frames, add a super now. Do not wait for the first super to be full before adding a second one. Bees that run out of room start preparing to leave. Bees that have room keep working.

attrs.title: The weekly inspection window
attrs.body: The egg-to-sealed-queen-cell cycle takes about eight days. Inspecting every seven days means you always catch open queen cells before they are sealed. One missed week in May can change swarm prevention into swarm control. If you cannot inspect in person, ask your local beekeeping association mentor to cover for you.

content.text: Step 2: Check every frame for queen cells
Remove the super and set it aside. Work through the brood box frame by frame. Look at the bottom edge and corners of each frame for

. These are large peanut-shaped cells pointing downwards from the comb face. Young, open queen cups with no egg in them are not yet swarm cells. If you find a cup with a tiny larva floating in royal jelly, that colony is committed to swarming. Act immediately.

If you find only empty queen cups and no eggs in them, knock them back with the hive tool on this visit. Check again in seven days. This buys time but is not a permanent fix. The colony will rebuild cups if the underlying pressure persists.
Step 3: Make an artificial swarm if cells contain eggs

satisfies the colony's urge to divide without losing bees. Find the old queen. Move her with the frame she is standing on into a new brood box on the original hive stand. Fill the new box with fresh frames. Move the original box to a new position at least 2 metres away. Add a new floor and roof. Flying bees returning from foraging come back to the original stand, boosting the new box with the old queen. The original box keeps the queen cells and raises a new queen.

Keep only one queen cell in the original box. Choose the longest, most evenly shaped cell on the best-placed frame. Destroy all other cells. If you leave multiple cells, the colony may cast secondary swarms as each virgin queen emerges.
What the bees do on the day you see a play flight
A busy afternoon cloud of bees in front of the hive entrance, facing inwards, is a

by young bees learning the hive location. This is not a swarm. A swarm flies upwards and away and does not return in a few minutes. If in doubt, stand back for ten minutes. If the cloud disperses and the bees re-enter the hive, it was orientation flying.

entries.cue: Found sealed queen cells, cannot find the old queen
entries.cause: The old queen may have already left with a prime swarm earlier that day
entries.fix: Listen at the entrance. A large swarm leaving sounds like a roar. If the colony is calmer than usual and the population looks reduced, the swarm has already gone. Treat the remaining box as swarm control: leave the best sealed cell, remove the others, wait for the new queen to mate.

entries.cue: Colony refills queen cups within days of removing them
entries.cause: The swarm impulse is strong; space or genetics are the real driver
entries.fix: Adding a second super and moving one frame of open brood into it (without the queen) can relieve pressure temporarily. If the same colony sarms repeatedly, requeening with a less swarmy strain is the long-term answer.

entries.cause: A warm spell accelerated cell development; the cell you missed was further along than it appeared
entries.fix: Collect the swarm if possible. Treat the original box as above. Increase inspection frequency to every five to six days during hot weather in May and June.
